original_abstract: Direct electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-derived intermediates offers a sustainable and safer alternative to conventional industrial processes that rely on hazardous chlorine or peroxide reagents. In this study, we report an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtOₓ/C) capable of achieving a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% for propylene oxide production at 50 mA cm⁻² under ambient conditions. By embedding platinum into the palladium oxide lattice, we successfully stabilized oxidized platinum species, significantly enhancing the catalyst’s overall performance and durability. Kinetic analyses indicate that epoxidation on the PdPtOₓ/C surface proceeds via an electrophilic attack by metal-bound peroxo intermediates. These findings establish a robust strategy for the selective electrochemical transfer of oxygen atoms directly from water, providing a versatile platform for diverse oxygenation reactions without the need for chemical mediators.
humanized_abstract: Direct electrochemical propylene epoxidation using water-derived intermediates represents a less hazardous and more sustainable route to propylene oxide production than the current industrial method that utilizes harmful chlorine or peroxide species. Here, an oxidized palladium-platinum alloy catalyst (PdPtOₓ/C) that affords a Faradaic efficiency of 66 ± 5% at a current density of 50 mA cm⁻² at ambient pressure is reported. By incorporating platinum into palladium oxide lattice, oxidized platinum species can be stabilized, leading to a superior catalyst stability and performance. The selectivity and stability of the catalyst were confirmed by quantitative kinetic analysis of the epoxidation reaction on PdPtOₓ/C surface, suggesting an electrophilic mechanism that proceeds through the formation of reactive metal-bound peroxo species. In summary, this work provides a sustainable solution to catalyze the selective electrochemical transfer of oxygen atom from water to perform various oxidative transformations in the absence of any catalyst activator.

The selectivity and stability of the catalyst were confirmed by quantitative kinetic analysis of the epoxidation reaction on PdPtOₓ/C surface, suggesting an electrophilic mechanism that proceeds through the formation of reactive metal-bound peroxo species. In summary, this work provides a sustainable solution to catalyze the selective electrochemical transfer of oxygen atom from water to perform various oxidative transformations in the absence of any catalyst activator.
